performance.name: Function on Indexed Column
performance.description: Detects functions applied to columns in WHERE predicates (e.g. WHERE LOWER(email) = ...).
performance.impact: Prevents index usage, forces full table scan
performance.fix_guidance: Use functional indexes or rewrite predicate without wrapping function

performance.name: OR in WHERE Clause
performance.description: Detects OR conditions in WHERE clauses which can prevent index usage.
performance.impact: OR conditions can prevent index usage depending on the query planner
performance.fix_guidance: Consider rewriting as UNION ALL of two queries

performance.name: ILIKE Disables Index
performance.impact: ILIKE performs case-insensitive matching but cannot use standard B-tree indexes. This causes full table scans on large tables. A query on a million-row table with ILIKE can be 1000x slower than with a proper index.
performance.fix_guidance: Create a citext column type or a functional index: CREATE INDEX idx ON table (lower(column)). Then use: WHERE lower(column) LIKE lower('value%'). Or use pg_trgm extension with GIN index for arbitrary substring matching.

performance.name: Deep Offset Pagination
performance.description: Detects OFFSET values over 1000 which degrade pagination performance.
performance.impact: Database must scan and discard all rows before the offset
performance.fix_guidance: Use keyset/cursor pagination instead: WHERE id > last_seen_id LIMIT n

performance.name: Cartesian Product (CROSS JOIN)
performance.description: Detects CROSS JOIN usage which produces a Cartesian product of rows.
performance.impact: Produces row count = table1_rows * table2_rows, exponential cost
performance.fix_guidance: Add explicit JOIN condition or use INNER JOIN

performance.name: Excessive Joins
performance.description: Detects queries with 5 or more JOIN clauses.
performance.impact: High join count increases query plan complexity and memory usage
performance.fix_guidance: Consider breaking into CTEs or denormalizing hot query paths

performance.name: Unfiltered Aggregation
performance.description: Detects COUNT(*), SUM(), AVG() without a WHERE clause on SELECT.
performance.impact: Aggregates entire table, expensive on large datasets
performance.fix_guidance: Add WHERE clause to filter rows before aggregation

performance.name: ORDER BY in Subquery
performance.description: Detects ORDER BY inside a subquery or CTE where it is typically meaningless.
performance.impact: ORDER BY in subquery is meaningless and wastes sort cost
performance.fix_guidance: Remove ORDER BY from subquery unless paired with LIMIT/TOP

performance.name: Unbounded SELECT
performance.description: Detects SELECT statements with no LIMIT clause on non-aggregated queries.
performance.impact: May return millions of rows, overwhelming application memory
performance.fix_guidance: Add LIMIT clause for paginated or exploratory queries

performance.name: NOT IN Subquery
performance.description: Detects NOT IN with subquery which can fail silently with NULLs.
performance.impact: NOT IN with subquery fails silently with NULLs and disables index usage
performance.fix_guidance: Rewrite as NOT EXISTS or LEFT JOIN ... WHERE col IS NULL

performance.name: Implicit Type Conversion on Indexed Column
performance.description: Detects comparisons where column and value types likely mismatch, forcing implicit conversion that prevents index usage.

performance.name: Composite Index Column Order Violation
performance.description: Detects WHERE clauses that filter on non-leading columns of common composite index patterns, preventing index seek.

performance.name: Non-SARGable OR Condition
performance.description: Detects OR conditions across different columns that prevent index usage (non-SARGable).

performance.name: COALESCE/ISNULL/NVL on Indexed Column
performance.description: Detects COALESCE, ISNULL, NVL, or IFNULL wrapping indexed columns in WHERE clause, which prevents index usage.
performance.impact: Wrapping a column in COALESCE/ISNULL forces evaluation of every row. WHERE ISNULL(status, 'x') = 'active' cannot use an index on status.
performance.fix_guidance: Handle NULL explicitly: WHERE (status = 'active' OR (status IS NULL AND 'x' = 'active')). Or use a filtered index, computed column, or ensure column is NOT NULL.

performance.name: Negation on Indexed Column (NOT, !=, <>)
performance.description: Detects NOT, !=, <> conditions that typically cannot use indexes efficiently.

performance.name: Table Lock Hint
performance.description: Detects table-level lock hints that can cause severe blocking under concurrency.
performance.impact: Table-level locks (TABLOCK, TABLOCKX) block ALL concurrent access to the table. Under load, this creates cascading waits that can freeze the entire application.
performance.fix_guidance: Remove table lock hints unless absolutely necessary. Use row-level locking (default behavior).

performance.name: NOLOCK / Read Uncommitted Hint
performance.description: Detects NOLOCK or READ UNCOMMITTED hints that can return inconsistent data.
performance.impact: NOLOCK reads uncommitted data (dirty reads), can skip rows, read rows twice, or return phantom data. It's not 'faster' — it's 'wrong'.
performance.fix_guidance: Use READ COMMITTED SNAPSHOT ISOLATION (RCSI) for non-blocking reads without dirty reads.

performance.name: Long Transaction Pattern
performance.description: Detects patterns indicating potentially long-running transactions that hold locks.
performance.impact: Long transactions hold locks for their entire duration, blocking other queries. A 10-second transaction holding a lock can queue up hundreds of waiting requests.
performance.fix_guidance: Keep transactions as short as possible. Do all preparation BEFORE BEGIN TRAN. Use optimistic concurrency.

performance.name: Missing Transaction Isolation Level
performance.description: Detects explicit transactions without isolation level specification, relying on default behavior.

performance.name: Cursor Declaration
performance.description: Detects CURSOR declarations, which indicate row-by-row processing (RBAR - Row By Agonizing Row).
performance.impact: Cursors process one row at a time, requiring round-trips and preventing set-based optimizations. Cursor operations are typically 10-100x slower than equivalent set-based SQL.
performance.fix_guidance: Rewrite using set-based operations: UPDATE...FROM, MERGE, window functions. If cursor is truly necessary, use FAST_FORWARD READ_ONLY.

performance.name: WHILE Loop Pattern
performance.description: Detects WHILE loops that may indicate row-by-row processing.
performance.impact: WHILE loops in SQL often indicate procedural thinking applied to a set-based language. Each iteration may execute separate queries, multiplying execution time.
performance.fix_guidance: Replace WHILE loops with set-based operations. Use recursive CTEs for hierarchical processing.

performance.name: Nested Loop Join Hint
performance.description: Detects LOOP JOIN hints that force nested loop joins, often inappropriate for large datasets.
performance.impact: Forced nested loop joins perform O(n*m) comparisons. For large tables, this is catastrophic. The optimizer usually knows better.
performance.fix_guidance: Remove join hints and let the optimizer choose. If hint is necessary, document why.

performance.name: Large IN Clause
performance.description: Detects IN clauses with excessive values (>50) that can cause memory pressure and poor plans.

performance.name: Unbounded Temp Table Creation
performance.description: Detects SELECT INTO temp table without WHERE clause or row limit.
performance.impact: Unbounded SELECT INTO can fill tempdb, crash the instance, or exhaust memory. A single runaway query can impact all database users.
performance.fix_guidance: Always add WHERE clause or TOP/LIMIT to bound result size. Pre-create temp table with explicit schema for better memory estimation.

performance.name: ORDER BY Without LIMIT in Subquery
performance.description: Detects ORDER BY in subqueries/CTEs without TOP/LIMIT, which is meaningless and wastes resources.

performance.name: GROUP BY on High-Cardinality Expression
performance.description: Detects GROUP BY on columns likely to have high cardinality (timestamps, IDs, UUIDs).

performance.name: Query Optimizer Hint
performance.description: Detects query hints that override optimizer decisions.
performance.impact: Query hints freeze execution plans. As data grows and distribution changes, hinted plans become suboptimal. Hints hide underlying issues (missing indexes, bad statistics).
performance.fix_guidance: Remove hints and fix root cause: update statistics, add indexes, simplify query.

performance.name: Index Hint
performance.description: Detects forced index usage hints.
performance.impact: Index hints force specific index usage regardless of statistics. When data changes, the forced index may become suboptimal, but the hint remains.
performance.fix_guidance: Let the optimizer choose indexes. If it chooses wrong, update statistics or create better indexes.

performance.name: Parallel Query Hint
performance.description: Detects MAXDOP hints that override server parallelism settings.
performance.impact: MAXDOP hints override server-level parallelism. MAXDOP 1 forces single-threaded execution. High MAXDOP values can starve other queries of CPU.
performance.fix_guidance: Use server or database-level MAXDOP settings. Per-query hints are rarely justified.

performance.name: Scalar UDF in SELECT/WHERE
performance.description: Detects scalar user-defined function calls in SELECT or WHERE clauses.
performance.impact: Scalar UDFs execute row-by-row, prevent parallelism, and cannot be inlined in most SQL versions. A single scalar UDF can make queries 100x slower.
performance.fix_guidance: Rewrite as inline table-valued function (iTVF) or move logic to application layer.

performance.name: Correlated Subquery
performance.description: Detects correlated subqueries that execute once per outer row.

performance.name: ORDER BY on Non-Indexed Column
performance.description: Detects ORDER BY on columns unlikely to be indexed, forcing expensive sorts.

performance.name: Large Unbatched Operation
performance.description: Detects UPDATE/DELETE without WHERE clause or row limit, affecting entire tables.

performance.name: Missing Batch Size in Loop
performance.description: Detects WHILE loops with UPDATE/DELETE that don't specify TOP/LIMIT.
performance.impact: WHILE loops without batch limits may process unlimited rows per iteration, negating the benefits of batching.
performance.fix_guidance: Always use TOP/LIMIT in batched operations inside loops.

performance.name: Excessive Column Count in SELECT
performance.description: Detects SELECT statements with more than 20 explicit columns.

performance.name: Large Object Column in Non-Filtered Query
performance.description: Detects SELECT of BLOB/CLOB/TEXT columns without WHERE clause, potentially transferring massive data.

performance.name: Missing SET NOCOUNT ON
performance.description: T-SQL stored procedures without SET NOCOUNT ON send row count messages for every DML statement back to the client, generating unnecessary network traffic and interfering with some ORMs and client libraries.
performance.impact: Each INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE inside the procedure sends a 'N rows affected' message to the client. This adds network overhead and can cause ADO.NET and other drivers to misinterpret result sets.
performance.fix_guidance: Add SET NOCOUNT ON as the first statement in every stored procedure and trigger. This is a universal T-SQL best practice.

performance.name: Unfiltered COUNT(*) — Consider reltuples
performance.description: COUNT(*) without WHERE requires a full sequential scan in PostgreSQL.
performance.impact: COUNT(*) on a large table without WHERE scans every row.
performance.fix_guidance: For approximate counts use: SELECT reltuples FROM pg_class WHERE relname = 'table'.

performance.name: NOT IN With Potentially NULLable Subquery
performance.description: NOT IN returns no rows if any value in the subquery result is NULL.
performance.impact: A single NULL in the subquery result causes NOT IN to return zero rows.
performance.fix_guidance: Replace NOT IN (SELECT ...) with NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM ... WHERE ...).

performance.name: SELECT FOR UPDATE Without NOWAIT/SKIP LOCKED (PG)
performance.description: SELECT FOR UPDATE without NOWAIT or SKIP LOCKED blocks indefinitely.
performance.impact: Without NOWAIT, the query blocks until the lock is released.
performance.fix_guidance: Add NOWAIT to fail immediately or SKIP LOCKED to skip locked rows.

performance.name: SELECT FOR UPDATE Without LIMIT (MySQL)
performance.description: SELECT FOR UPDATE without LIMIT can escalate to table-level lock in InnoDB.
performance.impact: Locking too many rows blocks concurrent writes.
performance.fix_guidance: Add LIMIT to restrict locked rows. Use indexed WHERE clause.

performance.name: ORDER BY RAND() Full Table Sort
performance.description: ORDER BY RAND() generates a random value for every row then sorts all rows.
performance.impact: On 1M rows, ORDER BY RAND() LIMIT 1 still reads and sorts all 1M rows.
performance.fix_guidance: Use random offset subquery or maintain a sampling column.

performance.name: FORCE INDEX / USE INDEX Hint
performance.description: FORCE INDEX overrides the optimizer. May become suboptimal as data changes.
performance.impact: Forced indexes bypass the optimizer and may force worse plans over time.
performance.fix_guidance: Remove index hints. Update statistics with ANALYZE TABLE instead.

performance.name: SELECT INTO Temp Table Without Index
performance.description: SELECT INTO creates a temp table without indexes, causing table scans.
performance.impact: Temp tables without indexes cause table scans on every join or filter.
performance.fix_guidance: Add CREATE INDEX after SELECT INTO, or pre-create the table with indexes.

performance.name: Implicit Conversion in JOIN Predicate
performance.description: Explicit CONVERT/CAST in JOIN suggests type mismatch preventing index usage.
performance.impact: Implicit conversion prevents index seeks, forcing table scans.
performance.fix_guidance: Ensure JOIN columns have matching data types at the schema level.

performance.name: SELECT FOR UPDATE Without NOWAIT (Oracle)
performance.description: SELECT FOR UPDATE without NOWAIT blocks indefinitely in Oracle.
performance.impact: Without NOWAIT, the session hangs waiting for row locks.
performance.fix_guidance: Add NOWAIT to raise ORA-00054 or SKIP LOCKED (Oracle 11g+).

performance.name: VARIANT Column in WHERE Without CAST
performance.description: VARIANT columns in WHERE without CAST prevent micro-partition pruning.
performance.impact: Without CAST, Snowflake scans all micro-partitions.
performance.fix_guidance: Cast VARIANT: WHERE data:field::STRING = 'value'.

performance.name: ORDER BY on VARIANT Column
performance.description: Sorting on VARIANT requires runtime type resolution — slower than typed columns.
performance.impact: VARIANT sorting inspects type per row, adding significant overhead.
performance.fix_guidance: Cast in ORDER BY: ORDER BY data:field::NUMBER.

performance.name: SELECT DISTINCT on UNNEST
performance.description: DISTINCT on UNNEST forces a full shuffle of unnested data — expensive.
performance.impact: UNNEST explodes arrays then DISTINCT shuffles all rows to deduplicate.
performance.fix_guidance: Use ARRAY_AGG(DISTINCT ...) instead. Filter before UNNEST to reduce volume.

performance.name: REGEXP on Large Table Without Filter
performance.description: REGEXP functions are CPU-intensive and run on every row without a WHERE pre-filter.
performance.impact: REGEXP on every row consumes slot time and increases cost.
performance.fix_guidance: Pre-filter with LIKE or STARTS_WITH. Use partitioned/clustered tables.

performance.name: Consider WAL Mode for Concurrent Access
performance.description: SQLite defaults to journal_mode=DELETE which locks the entire database on writes. WAL (Write-Ahead Logging) mode allows concurrent readers during writes.
performance.impact: Without WAL mode, any write operation locks the entire database file, blocking all concurrent readers and writers.
performance.fix_guidance: Set PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL for concurrent read/write access. Note: WAL mode is persistent and only needs to be set once.

performance.name: LIKE Without COLLATE NOCASE
performance.description: SQLite's LIKE operator is case-insensitive for ASCII by default, but indexes are case-sensitive. Using COLLATE NOCASE on the column definition allows indexes to be used with LIKE.
performance.impact: Without COLLATE NOCASE on the column, LIKE queries cannot use indexes and fall back to full table scan.
performance.fix_guidance: Define columns with COLLATE NOCASE: CREATE TABLE t (name TEXT COLLATE NOCASE). Or create an index with COLLATE NOCASE.

performance.name: SELECT * on Redshift Columnar Storage
performance.description: Redshift stores data in a columnar format. SELECT * reads every column from disk even if only a few are needed, wasting I/O and network bandwidth.
performance.impact: Redshift charges for bytes scanned. SELECT * on a 100-column table reads 100x more data than selecting the 1 column needed.
performance.fix_guidance: Always specify explicit column names. Use CTAS or views to reduce column exposure.

performance.name: ORDER BY Without LIMIT on Redshift
performance.description: ORDER BY without LIMIT on Redshift requires redistributing all rows to a single node for sorting, causing massive network traffic and memory pressure on the leader node.
performance.impact: All rows must be sent to the leader node for global sorting. On large tables this can OOM the leader or take very long.
performance.fix_guidance: Add LIMIT to bound the result. For analytics, use window functions with PARTITION BY to sort within partitions.

performance.name: NOT IN on Redshift (Hash Join Explosion)
performance.description: NOT IN on Redshift generates a nested loop anti-join or large hash table. With NULLs in the subquery, Redshift cannot optimize this and must scan all rows.
performance.impact: NOT IN forces Redshift to build a hash table of the entire subquery result. With NULLs present, it degrades to a nested loop.
performance.fix_guidance: Replace NOT IN (SELECT ...) with NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM ... WHERE ...) or LEFT JOIN ... WHERE key IS NULL.

performance.name: SELECT Without PREWHERE
performance.description: ClickHouse PREWHERE filters data before reading non-filtered columns, dramatically reducing I/O on wide MergeTree tables. WHERE reads all columns first then filters.
performance.impact: Without PREWHERE, ClickHouse reads all columns from disk before filtering. PREWHERE reads only the filter column first, skipping granules that don't match.
performance.fix_guidance: Move selective filter conditions from WHERE to PREWHERE. ClickHouse auto-optimizes simple conditions, but explicit PREWHERE guarantees it.

performance.name: JOIN Without GLOBAL on Distributed Table
performance.description: On ClickHouse distributed tables, JOIN without GLOBAL sends the right-side subquery to every shard independently. GLOBAL JOIN executes it once and broadcasts the result.
performance.impact: Without GLOBAL, each shard executes the right-side subquery independently. For N shards this means N redundant executions.
performance.fix_guidance: Use GLOBAL JOIN or GLOBAL IN for distributed subqueries: SELECT * FROM dist_table GLOBAL JOIN (SELECT ...) USING key.

performance.name: COPY Without FORMAT Specification
performance.description: DuckDB's COPY command infers format from file extension, but explicit FORMAT specification avoids ambiguity and ensures correct parsing especially for extensionless paths or URLs.
performance.impact: Without FORMAT, DuckDB guesses from the file extension. For URLs, pipes, or extensionless files this can silently fail.
performance.fix_guidance: Add FORMAT explicitly: COPY t FROM 'file.csv' (FORMAT CSV). Supported: CSV, PARQUET, JSON.

performance.name: Large IN List — Use VALUES Table
performance.description: Large IN (...) lists in DuckDB are slower than using a VALUES table expression with a JOIN. DuckDB optimizes VALUES as a scan operator.
performance.impact: Large IN lists are evaluated as repeated OR conditions. A VALUES table with a semi-join is more efficient for DuckDB's vectorized engine.
performance.fix_guidance: Replace IN (1,2,...,100) with: JOIN (VALUES (1),(2),...,(100)) AS v(id) USING (id).

performance.name: Implicit Cross-Join on Distributed Engine
performance.description: Comma-separated tables in FROM without JOIN conditions create implicit cross-joins. On Presto/Trino this shuffles data across all workers, generating O(n*m) intermediate rows.
performance.impact: Cross-joins on distributed engines shuffle all data. Two 1M-row tables produce 1 trillion intermediate rows across workers.
performance.fix_guidance: Replace comma-separated tables with explicit JOIN: FROM a JOIN b ON a.key = b.key.

performance.name: ORDER BY Without LIMIT on Distributed Engine
performance.description: ORDER BY without LIMIT on Presto/Trino requires gathering all data to the coordinator for global sorting, causing OOM on large result sets.
performance.impact: All rows are sent to the coordinator node for sorting. This can exhaust coordinator memory and crash the query.
performance.fix_guidance: Add LIMIT to bound the result. For analytics use window functions with PARTITION BY to sort within partitions.

performance.name: BROADCAST Hint on Large Table
performance.description: /*+ BROADCAST(table) */ forces Spark to broadcast the entire table to all executors. If the table is large, this causes OOM on executors and driver.
performance.impact: Broadcasting a table larger than spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold (default 10MB) causes OOM. The entire table is serialized and sent to every executor.
performance.fix_guidance: Remove BROADCAST hint and let Spark auto-decide. If needed, verify table size is under autoBroadcastJoinThreshold. Use EXPLAIN to confirm join strategy.

performance.name: UDF in WHERE Prevents Pushdown
performance.description: User-defined functions (UDFs) in WHERE clauses prevent Spark from pushing predicates down to the data source. This forces Spark to read all data then filter in-memory.
performance.impact: Without pushdown, Spark reads the entire table/partition from storage. For Parquet/Delta files this bypasses column pruning and row group filtering.
performance.fix_guidance: Replace UDFs with built-in Spark SQL functions which support pushdown. If UDF is necessary, pre-filter with pushable predicates first.

performance.name: HAVING Without GROUP BY
performance.description: HAVING without GROUP BY treats the entire result set as a single group. This is valid SQL but almost always a mistake — the developer likely forgot GROUP BY, and the query returns at most one row.
performance.impact: Without GROUP BY, HAVING filters the entire table as one group. The query returns either 0 or 1 rows, which is rarely intended.
performance.fix_guidance: Add the missing GROUP BY clause, or use WHERE instead of HAVING if no aggregation is needed.

performance.name: LEFT JOIN With IS NOT NULL Filter
performance.description: A LEFT JOIN followed by WHERE right_table.column IS NOT NULL is logically equivalent to an INNER JOIN but prevents the optimizer from choosing the most efficient join strategy.
performance.impact: The LEFT JOIN preserves unmatched rows, then WHERE immediately removes them. This wastes I/O and prevents join reordering.
performance.fix_guidance: Replace LEFT JOIN with INNER JOIN when the WHERE clause filters out NULLs from the right table. The result is identical but the optimizer has more freedom.

performance.name: GROUP BY Implicit Sort (Removed in MySQL 8.0)
performance.description: Before MySQL 8.0, GROUP BY implicitly sorted results. In 8.0+ this was removed. Code relying on GROUP BY order without explicit ORDER BY produces non-deterministic results on MySQL 8.0+.
performance.impact: Results appear sorted on MySQL 5.x but are unordered on 8.0+. This causes subtle bugs during MySQL version upgrades.
performance.fix_guidance: Add explicit ORDER BY if sorted output is needed. If order doesn't matter, add ORDER BY NULL to explicitly disable sorting.

performance.name: WAITFOR DELAY in Production Code
performance.description: WAITFOR DELAY pauses execution for the specified duration. In production code this is almost always a testing artifact or a blind SQL injection indicator.
performance.impact: WAITFOR DELAY ties up a connection and worker thread for the specified duration. An attacker can use it to confirm blind SQL injection or exhaust the connection pool.
performance.fix_guidance: Remove WAITFOR DELAY from production code. If used for polling, use Service Broker or Query Notification instead.

performance.name: ClickHouse Mutation (ALTER UPDATE/DELETE)
performance.description: ClickHouse mutations (ALTER TABLE UPDATE/DELETE) are heavy asynchronous operations that rewrite entire data parts. They are not designed for frequent row-level modifications.
performance.impact: Mutations rewrite entire data parts asynchronously. Frequent mutations queue up and consume disk I/O and CPU. They are not transactional and cannot be rolled back.
performance.fix_guidance: Use ReplacingMergeTree or CollapsingMergeTree for logical deletes/updates. Reserve ALTER TABLE mutations for rare bulk corrections.

reliability.name: Catastrophic Data Loss Risk
reliability.description: Detects DELETE or UPDATE without WHERE clause.

reliability.name: INSERT IGNORE Silences Errors
reliability.impact: INSERT IGNORE silently discards duplicate key errors, data truncation warnings, and constraint violations. Failed inserts are invisible — data loss goes undetected.
reliability.fix_guidance: Use INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE for intentional upserts. Use explicit duplicate checking for insert-only logic. Never use INSERT IGNORE where data integrity matters.

reliability.name: REPLACE INTO Deletes and Reinserts
reliability.impact: REPLACE INTO deletes the existing row and inserts a new one when a duplicate key is found. This resets AUTO_INCREMENT IDs, fires DELETE triggers unexpectedly, and breaks foreign key references silently.
reliability.fix_guidance: Use INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE instead. It updates only specified columns without deleting the row, preserving IDs, timestamps, and foreign key integrity.

reliability.name: Destructive Schema Change (DROP)
reliability.description: Detects DROP TABLE statements in code.

reliability.name: Truncate Without Transaction
reliability.description: Detects TRUNCATE TABLE statements outside of an explicit transaction context. TRUNCATE is non-transactional in many databases (MySQL, older PostgreSQL) and cannot be rolled back. Even in databases where it is transactional, it is rarely wrapped in a transaction in application code.
reliability.impact: TRUNCATE removes all rows instantly with no row-by-row logging, making recovery impossible without a backup in non-transactional databases.
reliability.fix_guidance: Wrap TRUNCATE in an explicit BEGIN/START TRANSACTION block with a subsequent COMMIT only after verification. Prefer DELETE with WHERE for recoverable operations. Use TRUNCATE only in controlled migration scripts.

reliability.name: ALTER TABLE Without Backup Signal
reliability.description: Detects destructive ALTER TABLE operations: DROP COLUMN, MODIFY COLUMN (type change), and RENAME COLUMN. These operations can cause irreversible data loss or application breakage if not coordinated with application deployments.
reliability.impact: DROP COLUMN permanently destroys column data. MODIFY COLUMN can silently truncate data if the new type is narrower. RENAME COLUMN breaks all application queries referencing the old name.
reliability.fix_guidance: Always take a full backup before destructive ALTER operations. Use expand-contract pattern for zero-downtime schema changes: add new column, migrate data, update application, then drop old column. Test in staging first.

reliability.name: Missing Transaction Rollback Handler
reliability.description: Detects BEGIN/START TRANSACTION blocks that have a COMMIT but no ROLLBACK, indicating missing error handling. Transactions without ROLLBACK leave the database in an inconsistent state if an error occurs mid-transaction.
reliability.impact: Without ROLLBACK, a failed transaction may partially commit changes, leaving data in an inconsistent state. This is especially dangerous for multi-step operations like financial transfers.
reliability.fix_guidance: Always pair BEGIN/COMMIT with a ROLLBACK in error handling. Use savepoints for partial rollbacks in complex transactions. In application code, use try/catch/finally patterns to ensure ROLLBACK on exception.

reliability.name: Autocommit Disable Detection
reliability.description: Detects explicit disabling of autocommit mode (SET autocommit = 0, SET IMPLICIT_TRANSACTIONS ON). When autocommit is disabled globally, every statement starts an implicit transaction that must be manually committed or rolled back, which can cause long-running locks and accidental data loss on connection drop.
reliability.impact: Disabling autocommit causes uncommitted changes to be silently rolled back on connection drop or application crash, leading to data loss. Long-running implicit transactions hold locks and degrade concurrency.
reliability.fix_guidance: Use explicit BEGIN/COMMIT blocks instead of disabling autocommit globally. If autocommit must be disabled, ensure every code path has explicit COMMIT or ROLLBACK. Monitor for long-running transactions via pg_stat_activity or information_schema.innodb_trx.

reliability.name: Swallowed Exception Pattern
reliability.description: Detects exception handling blocks that swallow errors silently: WHEN OTHERS THEN NULL (Oracle), EXCEPTION WHEN OTHERS THEN (PL/pgSQL with no RAISE), and empty CATCH blocks in T-SQL. Swallowed exceptions hide data integrity failures and make debugging impossible.
reliability.impact: Silent exception swallowing means failed operations appear to succeed. Data integrity violations, constraint failures, and deadlocks go undetected, leading to corrupted application state.
reliability.fix_guidance: Always re-raise or log exceptions. In Oracle PL/SQL use RAISE or RAISE_APPLICATION_ERROR. In PostgreSQL use RAISE EXCEPTION. In T-SQL use THROW or RAISERROR. Never use WHEN OTHERS THEN NULL in production code.

reliability.name: Missing Savepoint in Long Transaction
reliability.description: Detects long multi-statement transactions (containing 3 or more DML operations: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) without a SAVEPOINT. Long transactions without savepoints cannot be partially rolled back, forcing a full rollback on any error.
reliability.impact: A failure in step 10 of a 10-step transaction forces rollback of all previous steps. Savepoints allow partial recovery and reduce re-work cost.
reliability.fix_guidance: Use SAVEPOINT after logically complete sub-operations within long transactions. Use ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT to recover from partial failures without rolling back the entire transaction.

reliability.name: Non-Idempotent INSERT Pattern
reliability.description: Detects INSERT statements without ON CONFLICT/ON DUPLICATE KEY or unique constraint checks, which will fail or create duplicates on retry.
reliability.impact: Non-idempotent INSERTs cause duplicate data on network retries, application restarts, or message queue redelivery. This corrupts data and breaks business logic.
reliability.fix_guidance: Use idempotent patterns: INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING, INSERT IGNORE, INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE, or MERGE. Include unique identifiers (UUID) from the client.

reliability.name: Non-Idempotent UPDATE Pattern
reliability.description: Detects UPDATE statements using relative operations (+=, -=, counter++) without version checks, which produce different results on retry.
reliability.impact: Relative updates like SET count = count + 1 execute multiple times on retry, causing incorrect totals. Financial calculations become inaccurate, inventory goes negative.
reliability.fix_guidance: Use optimistic locking: UPDATE ... SET count = count + 1, version = version + 1 WHERE id = ? AND version = ?. Or use idempotency keys to track processed operations.

reliability.name: Read-Modify-Write Without Lock
reliability.description: Detects patterns suggesting read-modify-write cycles (SELECT followed by UPDATE on same table) without FOR UPDATE lock or transaction isolation.
reliability.impact: Read-modify-write without locks causes lost updates. Two concurrent transactions read the same value, both modify, both write — one update is lost. Classic race condition.
reliability.fix_guidance: Use SELECT ... FOR UPDATE to lock rows during read-modify-write. Or use SERIALIZABLE isolation. Better: use atomic UPDATE with single statement.

reliability.name: TOCTOU Pattern
reliability.description: Detects IF EXISTS / IF NOT EXISTS checks followed by INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE without proper locking, creating time-of-check-time-of-use vulnerabilities.
reliability.impact: TOCTOU vulnerabilities allow race conditions: checking if row exists, then acting, leaves a gap where another transaction can change state. Common in user registration, inventory management.
reliability.fix_guidance: Use atomic operations: INSERT ... ON CONFLICT, MERGE, or INSERT ... WHERE NOT EXISTS as single statement. If IF is required, wrap in SERIALIZABLE transaction or use advisory locks.

reliability.name: Orphan Record Risk
reliability.description: Detects INSERT statements referencing likely foreign key columns without verifying parent record existence.
reliability.impact: INSERTs without FK verification create orphan records when parent doesn't exist. If FK constraints are disabled or missing, data integrity is silently corrupted.
reliability.fix_guidance: Ensure FK constraints exist in schema. Or verify parent: INSERT INTO orders (user_id) SELECT ? WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM users WHERE id = ?). Use deferred FK checks if needed.

reliability.name: Cascade Delete Risk
reliability.description: Detects DELETE on parent tables that likely have cascading child records, risking unintended mass deletion.
reliability.impact: DELETE on parent table with ON DELETE CASCADE can wipe millions of child records in one statement. Often unintended and irreversible without backups.
reliability.fix_guidance: Check child records before DELETE: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM child_table WHERE parent_id = ?. Use soft delete (is_deleted flag). Disable CASCADE for critical tables. Require explicit confirmation.

reliability.name: Deadlock Pattern
reliability.description: Detects transactions that update multiple tables, which can cause deadlocks if other transactions update the same tables in different order.
reliability.impact: Deadlocks occur when Transaction A locks Table1 then waits for Table2, while Transaction B locks Table2 then waits for Table1. Both freeze, one must abort.
reliability.fix_guidance: Always lock tables in consistent alphabetical order across all transactions. Use SELECT ... FOR UPDATE in consistent order. Consider using NOWAIT and retry logic.

reliability.name: Lock Escalation Risk
reliability.description: Detects UPDATE/DELETE without WHERE clause or with unbounded conditions that may lock excessive rows, causing lock escalation and blocking.
reliability.impact: SQL Server escalates row locks to table locks after ~5000 locks. Wide UPDATE/DELETE statements lock the entire table, blocking all other operations.
reliability.fix_guidance: Add selective WHERE with indexed columns. Use TOP/LIMIT for batching. Consider ROWLOCK hint if table lock is not acceptable. Process in smaller batches.

reliability.name: Long-Running Query Risk
reliability.description: Detects queries with multiple JOINs, subqueries, and no LIMIT that may run indefinitely without timeout protection.
reliability.impact: Complex queries without bounds can run for hours, consuming connections, blocking resources, and exhausting timeout-less connection pools.
reliability.fix_guidance: Add LIMIT/TOP to bound result size. Set query timeout at connection level. Use query governor or Resource Governor. Monitor and kill long-running queries.

reliability.name: Stale Read Risk
reliability.description: Detects UPDATE/INSERT followed by immediate SELECT without transaction, which may return stale data in replicated/distributed environments.
reliability.impact: In replicated databases, writes go to primary, reads may hit replicas. SELECT immediately after UPDATE may return old data if replication lag exists.
reliability.fix_guidance: Wrap write-then-read in transaction. Use read-from-primary hints for critical reads. Use RETURNING/OUTPUT clause to get written data atomically. Accept eventual consistency where appropriate.

reliability.name: Missing Retry Logic
reliability.description: Detects transaction blocks without error handling or retry patterns, which will fail permanently on transient errors.
reliability.impact: Transactions fail on transient errors (deadlock, timeout, connection blip). Without retry logic, operations fail permanently when they could succeed on retry.
reliability.fix_guidance: Implement retry loop with exponential backoff for deadlocks (error 1205) and timeouts. Use TRY...CATCH block. Limit retry attempts (3-5). Log failures for monitoring.

reliability.name: COPY INTO Without Explicit File Format
reliability.impact: COPY INTO without FILE_FORMAT relies on default or stage-level format settings. If defaults change or the stage is reconfigured, data loading silently fails or loads malformed data without error.
reliability.fix_guidance: Always specify FILE_FORMAT explicitly: COPY INTO table FROM stage FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = 'CSV' FIELD_DELIMITER = ',').

reliability.name: MySQL utf8 Instead of utf8mb4
reliability.description: MySQL's utf8 charset is an alias for utf8mb3 which only supports characters up to 3 bytes. This silently truncates 4-byte Unicode characters such as emoji and some CJK characters. Use utf8mb4 instead.
reliability.impact: Data containing 4-byte Unicode characters (emoji, some CJK, mathematical symbols) will be silently truncated or rejected, causing data loss.
reliability.fix_guidance: Replace CHARACTER SET utf8 with CHARACTER SET utf8mb4 and COLLATE utf8_general_ci with COLLATE utf8mb4_general_ci (or utf8mb4_unicode_ci). Update connection charset settings.

reliability.name: @@IDENTITY Instead of SCOPE_IDENTITY()
reliability.description: @@IDENTITY returns the last identity value generated in ANY scope on the current connection, including values generated by triggers. This can return the wrong value when triggers insert into tables with identity columns.
reliability.impact: If a trigger on the target table inserts into another table with an identity column, @@IDENTITY returns the trigger's identity value instead of the intended one, causing silent data corruption.
reliability.fix_guidance: Use SCOPE_IDENTITY() to get the last identity value generated in the current scope. For parallel inserts use OUTPUT clause.

reliability.name: MERGE Without HOLDLOCK
reliability.description: In SQL Server the MERGE statement is not atomic by default. Without WITH (HOLDLOCK) on the target table, concurrent MERGE executions can cause primary key violations or lost updates due to a race between the matched/not-matched evaluation and the actual DML.
reliability.impact: Concurrent MERGE statements can both evaluate NOT MATCHED and attempt INSERT, causing duplicate key errors. Or both evaluate MATCHED and overwrite each other's updates.
reliability.fix_guidance: Add WITH (HOLDLOCK) to the MERGE target: MERGE INTO target WITH (HOLDLOCK) USING ... Or wrap in SERIALIZABLE transaction.

reliability.name: CONNECT BY Without NOCYCLE
reliability.description: Oracle's CONNECT BY clause for hierarchical queries can enter an infinite loop if the data contains cycles. Without the NOCYCLE keyword the query will run until it hits ORA-01436 or exhausts resources.
reliability.impact: A cyclic reference in hierarchical data causes CONNECT BY to loop indefinitely, consuming CPU and memory until the session is killed or the server runs out of resources.
reliability.fix_guidance: Add NOCYCLE keyword: CONNECT BY NOCYCLE PRIOR parent_id = id. Use CONNECT_BY_ISCYCLE pseudo-column to detect and handle cycles.

reliability.name: CREATE INDEX Without CONCURRENTLY
reliability.description: CREATE INDEX locks the table against writes. Use CONCURRENTLY to avoid this.
reliability.impact: On large tables, CREATE INDEX can lock writes for minutes.
reliability.fix_guidance: Use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY. Note: cannot run inside a transaction block.

reliability.name: ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN With Volatile DEFAULT
reliability.description: ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN with volatile DEFAULT (now(), random()) rewrites the entire table.
reliability.impact: A table rewrite on a large table locks it exclusively.
reliability.fix_guidance: Add column as NULLable first, backfill in batches, then add NOT NULL.

reliability.name: ON UPDATE CASCADE With Timestamp Column
reliability.description: ON UPDATE CASCADE combined with ON UPDATE CURRENT_TIMESTAMP can cause unexpected cascades.
reliability.impact: Timestamp auto-update on parent row triggers CASCADE to all children.
reliability.fix_guidance: Separate timestamp columns from foreign key relationships.

reliability.name: TRUNCATE in TRY Without CATCH
reliability.description: TRUNCATE inside BEGIN TRY without BEGIN CATCH silently swallows errors.
reliability.impact: If TRUNCATE fails, the error is not caught and subsequent statements execute on stale data.
reliability.fix_guidance: Always pair BEGIN TRY with BEGIN CATCH. Use THROW to re-raise errors.

reliability.name: ALTER TABLE MOVE Without REBUILD INDEX
reliability.description: ALTER TABLE MOVE invalidates all indexes. They must be rebuilt afterwards.
reliability.impact: After MOVE, all indexes become UNUSABLE — queries error or fall back to full scans.
reliability.fix_guidance: Follow ALTER TABLE MOVE with ALTER INDEX ... REBUILD for every index.

reliability.name: COPY INTO Without ON_ERROR
reliability.description: COPY INTO defaults to ABORT_STATEMENT on first error — set ON_ERROR explicitly.
reliability.impact: A single malformed row aborts the entire load.
reliability.fix_guidance: Add ON_ERROR = 'CONTINUE' or 'SKIP_FILE'. Use VALIDATION_MODE for rejected rows.

reliability.name: DML Without WHERE on BigQuery
reliability.description: UPDATE/DELETE without WHERE on BigQuery processes all partitions — expensive and irreversible.
reliability.impact: DML on all partitions is expensive. BigQuery does not support ROLLBACK.
reliability.fix_guidance: Always include WHERE with partition filter: WHERE _PARTITIONDATE = '2024-01-01'.

reliability.name: ALTER TABLE DROP COLUMN (SQLite Limitation)
reliability.description: ALTER TABLE DROP COLUMN was only added in SQLite 3.35.0 (2021). Older versions will error. Even in 3.35+ it has restrictions: cannot drop PRIMARY KEY, UNIQUE, or columns referenced by indexes.
reliability.impact: On SQLite < 3.35.0 this statement fails entirely. On 3.35+ it cannot drop primary key columns, unique columns, or columns referenced by indexes or foreign keys.
reliability.fix_guidance: For broad compatibility, use the 12-step ALTER TABLE process: CREATE new table, INSERT from old, DROP old, RENAME new. See https://www.sqlite.org/lang_altertable.html

reliability.name: COPY Without MANIFEST
reliability.description: COPY without MANIFEST loads all files matching the S3 prefix. This can accidentally load duplicate or unexpected files if new files appear in the prefix during or between loads.
reliability.impact: Without MANIFEST, any file matching the S3 prefix is loaded. Concurrent writes to the prefix can cause duplicate data or load partial files.
reliability.fix_guidance: Use COPY ... FROM 's3://bucket/manifest.json' MANIFEST to load only explicitly listed files. Generate manifest with UNLOAD or your ETL pipeline.

reliability.name: SELECT Without FINAL on ReplacingMergeTree
reliability.description: ReplacingMergeTree tables may contain duplicate rows until background merges complete. SELECT without FINAL returns unmerged duplicates, producing incorrect results.
reliability.impact: Queries return duplicate rows that should have been deduplicated. Aggregations like COUNT and SUM return inflated values.
reliability.fix_guidance: Add FINAL keyword: SELECT * FROM table FINAL WHERE ... Note: FINAL forces a merge at query time which adds latency. For high-throughput reads, use OPTIMIZE TABLE periodically.

reliability.name: INSERT OVERWRITE Without Partition
reliability.description: INSERT OVERWRITE without a partition specification in Presto/Trino replaces ALL data in the target table. This is almost always unintentional and causes catastrophic data loss.
reliability.impact: All existing data in the table is replaced. Without partition specification, a query meant to update one day's data destroys the entire table.
reliability.fix_guidance: Always specify partition: INSERT OVERWRITE table PARTITION (dt='2024-01-01'). Or use INSERT INTO for append-only semantics.

reliability.name: INSERT OVERWRITE Without Partition
reliability.description: INSERT OVERWRITE on Spark/Databricks without a partition predicate replaces ALL data in the target table. With static partition overwrite mode (default), this destroys the entire table.
reliability.impact: All existing data in the table is replaced. A query intended to update one partition destroys the entire table unless partitionOverwriteMode=dynamic is set.
reliability.fix_guidance: Specify partition: INSERT OVERWRITE TABLE t PARTITION (dt='2024-01-01'). Or enable dynamic mode: SET spark.sql.sources.partitionOverwriteMode=dynamic.

reliability.name: Empty Transaction Block
reliability.description: A BEGIN...COMMIT block without any DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, SELECT) between them acquires and releases a transaction lock for no purpose. This is usually a code generation bug or copy-paste error.
reliability.impact: Empty transactions acquire locks, write to WAL/transaction log, and consume connection slots for no purpose. In high-concurrency systems this adds unnecessary contention.
reliability.fix_guidance: Remove the empty BEGIN...COMMIT block, or add the intended DML statements between them.

reliability.name: MyISAM Engine Usage
reliability.description: MyISAM does not support transactions, crash recovery, or foreign keys. A crash during a write operation can corrupt the table permanently. InnoDB is the default and recommended engine.
reliability.impact: MyISAM tables can be corrupted by crashes, power failures, or killed queries. There is no automatic recovery — manual REPAIR TABLE is required and may lose data.
reliability.fix_guidance: Use ENGINE=InnoDB. Convert existing tables: ALTER TABLE t ENGINE=InnoDB.

reliability.name: PRAGMA AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION
reliability.description: PRAGMA AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION creates an independent transaction within the current one. Commits in the autonomous transaction are permanent even if the parent transaction rolls back, breaking transactional consistency.
reliability.impact: Data committed in an autonomous transaction survives parent rollback. This breaks the assumption that ROLLBACK undoes all changes, leading to data inconsistency.
reliability.fix_guidance: Only use AUTONOMOUS_TRANSACTION for audit logging where you intentionally want commits to persist. Never use for business data modifications.

reliability.name: PRAGMA foreign_keys = OFF
reliability.description: Disabling foreign key enforcement in SQLite allows orphan records and referential integrity violations. SQLite defaults to foreign keys OFF, so this pragma explicitly maintains that unsafe state.
reliability.impact: Without foreign key enforcement, INSERT and DELETE can create orphan records that violate data relationships. There is no automatic cleanup.
reliability.fix_guidance: Set PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON at connection startup. Note: this must be set per-connection, not per-database.

compliance.name: Potential PII Selection
compliance.description: Detects selection of common PII column names (email, ssn, password).
compliance.impact: Accessing PII requires audit logging and strict access controls under GDPR/CCPA.

compliance.name: Unencrypted Sensitive Column Definition
compliance.description: Detects CREATE TABLE statements defining columns with sensitive names (password, secret, token, ssn, credit_card, cvv, pin) using plain text types (VARCHAR, TEXT, CHAR) without encryption hints in the column name or comment.
compliance.impact: Storing sensitive values in plain text columns violates PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and GDPR requirements and exposes data if the database is compromised.
compliance.fix_guidance: Use application-level encryption before storing, or database-level transparent encryption. Consider column names like password_hash or token_encrypted to signal encrypted storage.

compliance.name: Missing Retention Policy Signal
compliance.description: Detects CREATE TABLE statements for tables with audit, log, history, or event naming patterns. Such tables typically require a data retention policy under GDPR Article 5(1)(e) and similar regulations but rarely have one enforced at the schema level.
compliance.impact: Indefinite retention of audit and log data violates GDPR storage limitation principles and increases breach exposure surface.
compliance.fix_guidance: Implement a documented retention policy. Use partitioning with scheduled partition drops, or a scheduled DELETE WHERE created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL. Document the retention period in a data inventory.

compliance.name: Potential Cross-Border Data Transfer
compliance.description: Detects use of DBLINK, foreign data wrappers (postgres_fdw, dblink), or OPENROWSET which may transfer personal data across database boundaries or geographic regions without adequate GDPR safeguards.
compliance.impact: Transferring personal data to foreign servers in non-adequate countries without SCCs or BCRs violates GDPR Chapter V and can result in significant fines.
compliance.fix_guidance: Document all cross-border data flows in your data inventory. Ensure Standard Contractual Clauses or Binding Corporate Rules are in place. Prefer data minimization — transfer only pseudonymized or anonymized data.

compliance.name: Right to Erasure — Verify Cascade Completeness
compliance.description: Detects DELETE statements on tables with user, customer, account, profile, or member naming. GDPR Article 17 requires complete erasure across all related tables. A single-table DELETE may leave PII in audit logs, backups, or related tables.
compliance.impact: Incomplete erasure leaves PII in related tables, audit logs, caches, and backups, violating GDPR Article 17 and exposing the organization to regulatory penalties.
compliance.fix_guidance: Implement cascading deletes or a dedicated erasure procedure that covers all related tables. Document which systems hold personal data and verify backup purge schedules. Consider pseudonymization as an alternative to deletion.

compliance.name: Audit Log Tampering Risk
compliance.description: Detects DELETE or UPDATE statements targeting audit, log, or event tables. Modifying audit logs undermines non-repudiation requirements under SOX, PCI-DSS 10.5, and HIPAA audit controls.
compliance.impact: Modifying audit logs violates regulatory non-repudiation requirements and may constitute evidence tampering. PCI-DSS 10.5 explicitly requires audit logs to be protected from modification.
compliance.fix_guidance: Audit tables should be append-only. Use INSERT-only permissions on log tables. Implement write-once storage for compliance archives. Use database roles to prevent UPDATE/DELETE on audit tables.

compliance.name: Marketing Insert Without Consent Signal
compliance.description: Detects INSERT INTO statements targeting marketing, newsletter, campaign, or mailing list tables. GDPR Article 7 requires documented consent before adding users to marketing lists. A bare INSERT with no consent reference is a compliance signal.
compliance.impact: Adding users to marketing lists without recorded consent violates GDPR Article 7 and ePrivacy Directive, exposing the organization to regulatory complaints and fines.
compliance.fix_guidance: Ensure consent is recorded in a consent management table before INSERT. Include consent_id or consent_timestamp as a required foreign key in marketing tables. Audit consent validity before each campaign.

compliance.name: PHI Access Without Audit Trail
compliance.description: Detects SELECT queries on healthcare-related tables/columns without corresponding audit logging pattern, violating HIPAA audit requirements (45 CFR § 164.312(b)).
compliance.impact: Lack of audit trails for PHI access prevents detection of unauthorized access and violates HIPAA Technical Safeguards, potentially leading to OCR investigations and significant civil money penalties.
compliance.fix_guidance: Ensure all queries accessing PHI are wrapped in a stored procedure or application service that performs mandatory audit logging. Consider using database-level Audit features (e.g., SQL Server Audit, Oracle Audit Vault).

compliance.name: PHI Minimum Necessary Violation
compliance.description: Detects SELECT * queries on PHI tables. HIPAA requires covered entities to make reasonable efforts to limit PHI to the minimum necessary to accomplish the intended purpose (45 CFR § 164.502(b)).
compliance.impact: Fetching all columns from healthcare tables often retrieves unnecessary protected health information, increasing the risk and scope of a potential data breach.
compliance.fix_guidance: Explicitly list only the columns required for the specific business function. Avoid using SELECT * on tables containing PHI.

compliance.name: Unencrypted PHI Transit Signal
compliance.description: Detects connection strings or configuration queries hinting at unencrypted PHI transit (e.g., SSL/TLS disabled in connection properties for healthcare databases).
compliance.impact: Transmitting PHI over unencrypted connections violates the HIPAA Security Rule regarding transmission security (45 CFR § 164.312(e)(1)) and exposes data to man-in-the-middle attacks.
compliance.fix_guidance: Enable SSL/TLS for all database connections. Update connection strings to use encrypt=true, sslmode=verify-full, or equivalent secure parameters.

compliance.name: PAN Exposure in SQL
compliance.description: Detects queries that select or store unmasked 16-digit credit card numbers (PAN). PCI-DSS Requirement 3.3 requires masking PAN when displayed.
compliance.impact: Unmasked PANs in logs, cache, or application output violate PCI-DSS and increase the risk of financial fraud and massive non-compliance fines.
compliance.fix_guidance: Mask PANs at the database level using Dynamic Data Masking or in the application layer. Only store the last 4 digits if full PAN is not required. Use tokenization services.

compliance.name: CVV Storage Violation
compliance.description: Detects INSERT or CREATE TABLE statements referencing CVV, CVC, or CID. PCI-DSS Requirement 3.2 strictly prohibits storage of card security codes after authorization, even if encrypted.
compliance.impact: Storing CVV/CVC is a major PCI-DSS violation. It makes the database a prime target for attackers, as stolen CVVs enable 'CNP' (Card Not Present) fraud.
compliance.fix_guidance: DELETE all columns and code that store CVV/CVC. These values must only be used during the real-time authorization process and never persisted to disk.

compliance.name: Data Retention Violation
compliance.description: Detects queries on transaction/cardholder tables without date filters or purge logic, potentially violating PCI-DSS Requirement 3.1.
compliance.impact: Keeping cardholder data longer than necessary increases risk and violates PCI data minimization principles. It expands the scope of investigations in case of breach.
compliance.fix_guidance: Implement automated purge scripts or partitioning to remove data older than the defined retention period. Always include date filters when querying large transactional datasets.

compliance.name: Financial Data Modification Without Change Tracking
compliance.description: Detects UPDATE or DELETE statements on financial tables (ledger, accounts, payments, salaries) without a comment or where clause containing a change reason or tracking ID (ticket, bug, ref), violating SOX internal control requirements.
compliance.impact: Untracked modifications to financial records violate Sarbanes-Oxley (SOX) Section 404 internal controls, potentially leading to audit failures and legal liabilities for public companies.
compliance.fix_guidance: Always include a change tracking reference (e.g., Jira ticket ID or change reason) in the query comment or as a mandatory field in the audit metadata columns.

compliance.name: Segregation of Duties Violation
compliance.description: Detects queries where the same user context is performing both 'Creator' and 'Approver' functions on financial transactions, signaling an SoD risk.
compliance.impact: SoD violations allow a single individual to initiate and approve a financial transaction, creating a significant risk of fraud and material misstatement.
compliance.fix_guidance: Enforce SoD at the application and database trigger level. Ensure that created_by and approved_by values are never the same for the same record.

compliance.name: Data Subject Request Without Completeness Check
compliance.description: Detects SELECT queries for user data export (GDPR Art. 15) that use broad filters but might miss related sensitive tables like logs or backups.
compliance.impact: Incomplete responses to Data Subject Access Requests (DSAR) violate GDPR Article 15, leading to regulatory complaints and potential fines from Data Protection Authorities.
compliance.fix_guidance: Verify that all sources of personal data, including logs, secondary profiles, and metadata, are included in the export query or process.

compliance.name: Consent Withdrawal Not Honored
compliance.description: Detects SELECT queries on personal data that do not filter for active consent (e.g., missing WHERE consent_withdrawn = 0).
compliance.impact: Failing to honor consent withdrawal violates GDPR Article 7. Continuing to process data after consent is revoked is a major non-compliance event.
compliance.fix_guidance: Always include a consent check in the WHERE clause when querying personal data for processing categories that require consent.

compliance.name: Do Not Sell Flag Not Checked
compliance.description: Detects queries targeting marketing or third-party sharing tables that do not check the CCPA 'Do Not Sell' (DNS) flag.
compliance.impact: Processing 'sale' of data for consumers who have opted out violates CCPA requirements, exposing the company to statutory damages and enforcement actions.
compliance.fix_guidance: Modify all queries that share or sell data to include a check for the do_not_sell flag. Ensure it's set to FALSE before including the record.

quality.name: Implicit Join Syntax
quality.description: Detects old-style implicit joins using commas in FROM clause.

quality.name: Incorrect NULL Comparison
quality.description: Detects comparisons using = NULL or != NULL (and <> NULL) instead of IS NULL or IS NOT NULL. In SQL, NULL = NULL evaluates to NULL (unknown), not TRUE, so these comparisons always return no rows.
quality.impact: Using = NULL or != NULL silently returns zero rows regardless of actual NULL values, causing data to appear missing and logic to fail without errors.
quality.fix_guidance: Replace '= NULL' with 'IS NULL' and '!= NULL' or '<> NULL' with 'IS NOT NULL'. Use COALESCE() if a default value is needed instead of NULL handling.

quality.name: SELECT Without FROM Clause
quality.description: Detects SELECT statements that compute constant expressions without a FROM clause (e.g., SELECT 1, SELECT NOW(), SELECT 'value'). While valid in some databases, this pattern is often used in test code or leftover debug statements that should not reach production.
quality.impact: Constant SELECT statements in application queries may indicate debug code left in production, test artifacts, or incomplete query construction.
quality.fix_guidance: Remove debug SELECT statements before deployment. If the constant expression is needed, use database-specific syntax like SELECT 1 FROM DUAL (Oracle) or ensure the intent is documented.

quality.name: Hardcoded Date Literal in Filter
quality.description: Detects hardcoded date strings in WHERE clauses (e.g., WHERE date = '2023-01-01'). Hardcoded dates create maintenance burden, break time-based logic silently as time passes, and are a common source of stale query bugs.
quality.impact: Hardcoded dates become stale and cause queries to return unexpected results or no results as time passes. They also prevent query plan reuse.
quality.fix_guidance: Replace hardcoded dates with parameterized values (?), bind variables (:date), or dynamic expressions like NOW(), CURRENT_DATE, or CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'.

quality.name: Wildcard in EXISTS Subquery
quality.description: Detects SELECT * inside EXISTS subqueries. While functionally equivalent to SELECT 1 in most databases, SELECT * in EXISTS causes the query planner to potentially enumerate columns unnecessarily and signals poor query craftsmanship.
quality.impact: SELECT * in EXISTS subqueries may prevent optimizer shortcuts in some databases and increases the surface area for column-level permission errors.
quality.fix_guidance: Replace 'EXISTS (SELECT * FROM ...)' with 'EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM ...)'. SELECT 1 clearly signals intent and is universally optimized.

quality.name: Duplicate WHERE Condition
quality.description: Detects WHERE clauses containing the same column compared to the same value twice with AND (e.g., WHERE status = 'active' AND status = 'active'). Duplicate conditions add noise, confuse readers, and may indicate a copy-paste error hiding a logic bug.
quality.impact: Duplicate conditions waste parser cycles and obscure intent. They often indicate a copy-paste error where the second condition should have been different (e.g., OR instead of AND, or a different value).
quality.fix_guidance: Remove the duplicate condition. If both conditions were intended to filter on different values, verify the logic — AND with two equal conditions on the same column always reduces to a single condition.

quality.name: UNION Without ALL — Implicit Deduplication
quality.description: Detects UNION without ALL. UNION performs an implicit DISTINCT which requires a sort or hash operation over the full result set. If duplicate elimination is not required, UNION ALL is significantly faster.
quality.impact: UNION deduplicates results using an expensive sort or hash operation. On large result sets this adds significant overhead compared to UNION ALL.
quality.fix_guidance: If the result sets cannot contain meaningful duplicates, replace UNION with UNION ALL. If deduplication is required, keep UNION and add a comment explaining why to prevent future 'optimization' regressions.

quality.name: Subquery Missing Alias
quality.description: Detects subqueries in FROM clauses that are not given an alias. Unaliased subqueries are rejected by most databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL) and always indicate incomplete or draft query construction.
quality.impact: Unaliased subqueries cause syntax errors in PostgreSQL and MySQL. Even where accepted, they make the query unreadable and unreferenceable in outer query clauses.
quality.fix_guidance: Add an alias after the closing parenthesis: FROM (SELECT ...) AS subquery_name. Choose a descriptive alias that reflects the subquery's purpose.

quality.name: Commented-Out SQL Code
quality.description: Detects inline SQL comments that appear to contain commented-out query fragments (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE following -- or inside /* */). Commented-out code is a code quality smell indicating dead code that should be removed or tracked in version control.
quality.impact: Commented-out code creates confusion about query intent, may hide dangerous statements, and bloats query logs.
quality.fix_guidance: Remove commented-out SQL fragments before deploying queries. Use version control to track historical query variants. If the code may be needed, move it to a migration or script file with context.

quality.name: Excessive CASE Nesting
quality.description: Detects CASE expressions nested more than 3 levels deep, which are hard to read, test, and maintain.
quality.impact: Deeply nested CASE statements are difficult to understand, test, and debug. Each nesting level doubles the cognitive load. Often indicates business logic that belongs in application layer.
quality.fix_guidance: Refactor to lookup table or create a user-defined function. Limit CASE to 2-3 levels maximum. Use early returns in functions.

quality.name: Excessive Subquery Nesting
quality.description: Detects subqueries nested more than 3 levels deep, indicating overly complex query structure.
quality.impact: Deeply nested subqueries are unreadable and hard to optimize. Each level makes query execution unpredictable. Often indicates poor query design that should use CTEs or temp tables.
quality.fix_guidance: Use Common Table Expressions (CTEs) to flatten query structure. Or break into temp tables. Maximum 2-3 levels for readability.

quality.name: God Query
quality.description: Detects queries with excessive clauses (10+ JOINs, complex WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY).
quality.impact: God queries try to do everything in one statement. They're slow, hard to optimize, impossible to test, and unmaintainable. Often leads to unpredictable performance.
quality.fix_guidance: Break into multiple focused queries. Use temp tables for intermediate results. Separate data retrieval from business logic. Aim for < 5 JOINs per query.

quality.name: Cyclomatic Complexity in Stored Procedure
quality.description: Detects stored procedures with high cyclomatic complexity (many IF/WHILE/CASE branches).
quality.impact: High cyclomatic complexity means many code paths, making testing exponentially harder. Bugs hide in untested branches. Overly complex logic is hard to maintain.
quality.fix_guidance: Extract complex logic into smaller functions. Use lookup tables instead of IF chains. Limit to 10 branches per procedure. Aim for cyclomatic complexity < 10.

quality.name: Missing dbt Ref
quality.description: Table references in dbt models should use {{ ref() }} or {{ source() }} instead of hardcoded names.

quality.name: Hardcoded Schema
quality.description: Avoid hardcoded schema names (e.g., myschema.mytable). Use dbt macros or source() instead.

quality.name: Long Query (Line Count)
quality.description: Detects queries longer than 50 lines, suggesting over-complexity.
quality.impact: Queries over 50 lines are hard to understand, review, and debug. Often indicates poor separation of concerns or missing abstraction layers.
quality.fix_guidance: Break into multiple queries or CTEs. Use views for complex joins. Extract repeated patterns into functions. Aim for queries under 30 lines.

quality.name: Inconsistent Table Naming
quality.description: Detects inconsistent table naming conventions, specifically mixing singular and plural names in the same query.
quality.impact: Inconsistent naming makes the schema harder to learn and navigate. It creates uncertainty for developers and often leads to bugs where the wrong table name is guessed.
quality.fix_guidance: Standardize on either singular (user) or plural (users) for all table names. Plural is common for collections, singular for entity definitions.

quality.name: Ambiguous Alias
quality.description: Detects overly short (1-2 chars) or generic aliases that hinder readability.
quality.impact: Single-letter aliases make complex queries impossible to read without constant referencing back to the source. They hide the semantic meaning of the data.
quality.fix_guidance: Use 3+ character descriptive aliases. Example: 'cust' for customers, 'emp' for employees. Avoid aliases like 'a', 'b', 't1'.

quality.name: Hungarian Notation in Names
quality.description: Detects Hungarian notation prefixing (e.g., str_name, i_id, tbl_users).
quality.impact: Hungarian notation is redundant in SQL as types are defined in schema. It makes renaming/typing changes harder and clutters the code with obsolete metaphors.
quality.fix_guidance: Remove type prefixes. Use 'name' instead of 'str_name', 'id' instead of 'int_id'. Database metadata already provides type information.

quality.name: Reserved Word as Identifier
quality.description: Detects use of SQL reserved words (ORDER, GROUP, TABLE, etc.) as identifiers.
quality.impact: Using reserved words forces the use of double quotes and can lead to syntax errors if quotes are missing. It also makes queries much harder to read.
quality.fix_guidance: Choose a non-reserved synonym. Use 'created_at' instead of 'DATE', 'sort_order' instead of 'ORDER', 'user_account' instead of 'USER'.

quality.name: ROWNUM Without ORDER BY
quality.impact: ROWNUM filters rows BEFORE ORDER BY is applied. SELECT * FROM t WHERE ROWNUM <= 10 ORDER BY date returns 10 arbitrary rows then sorts them — not the top 10 by date. Results are non-deterministic and change with optimizer plan.
quality.fix_guidance: Wrap in a subquery: SELECT * FROM (SELECT * FROM t ORDER BY date) WHERE ROWNUM <= 10. Or use the modern FETCH FIRST syntax: SELECT * FROM t ORDER BY date FETCH FIRST 10 ROWS ONLY.

quality.name: SELECT FROM DUAL in Application SQL
quality.impact: SELECT 1 FROM DUAL is Oracle-specific legacy syntax for selecting constants. It works but is non-portable and signals Oracle-specific code that cannot be migrated to PostgreSQL, MySQL, or other databases without modification.
quality.fix_guidance: Use SELECT 1 (no FROM clause) which works in most modern databases. Oracle 23c+ supports SELECT 1 without FROM DUAL.

quality.name: Missing Column Comments
quality.description: Detects table definitions missing COMMENT or DESCRIPTION metadata.
quality.impact: Missing comments mean the business meaning of columns must be reverse-engineered from code. This slows down onboarding and leads to data misuse.
quality.fix_guidance: Add COMMENT 'description' to all column definitions. Explain units (e.g., 'price in USD') and expected values.

quality.name: Magic Constant Without Comment
quality.description: Detects hardcoded literals used in filters without inline comments.
quality.impact: Magic constants like 'STATUS_42' or 1001 represent business logic that is opaque to future maintainers. Without comments, it's impossible to know if the value is correct.
quality.fix_guidance: Add an inline comment explaining what the constant represents. E.g., WHERE status = 'A' -- A = Active.

quality.name: Complex Logic Without Explanation
quality.description: Detects long queries (>20 lines) that have no comments.
quality.impact: Queries over 20 lines without comments are 'write-only' code. They are prohibitively expensive to modify or peer-review safely.
quality.fix_guidance: Add header comments explaining the query's goal. Use inline comments for complex JOIN conditions or business logic branches.

quality.name: Missing Primary Key
quality.description: Detects table definitions missing a PRIMARY KEY constraint.
quality.impact: Tables without primary keys are a major design flaw. They prevent row uniqueness, break replication, make updates slow, and hinder most database optimizations.
quality.fix_guidance: Add a PRIMARY KEY to the table. Usually an auto-incrementing ID or a UUID. Every table must have a unique identifier.

quality.name: Implicit Foreign Key (Logic)
quality.description: Detects columns following *_id pattern missing explicit FOREIGN KEY constraints.
quality.impact: Missing foreign keys lead to orphaned records and data corruption. Referenced data can be deleted without cleaning up dependent rows, breaking application logic.
quality.fix_guidance: Add FOREIGN KEY ... REFERENCES ... constraints. This ensures referential integrity at the database level.

quality.name: Missing Index on Foreign Key
quality.description: Detects foreign key columns that lack a corresponding INDEX.
quality.impact: JOINs and CASCADE deletes on unindexed foreign keys are extremely slow. They cause full table scans for every referenced record lookup, killing performance.
quality.fix_guidance: Create an INDEX on the foreign key column. Most databases do not index FKs automatically. Example: CREATE INDEX idx_users_id ON profiles(user_id).

quality.name: Float for Currency
quality.description: Detects use of approximate types (FLOAT, REAL) for monetary values.
quality.impact: Float/Double types use binary floating-point math which leads to rounding errors (e.g., 0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3). This is catastrophic for financial data.
quality.fix_guidance: Use DECIMAL or NUMERIC for currency. Or store as integer (cents/pence). Never use floating point types for money.

quality.name: Non-Deterministic Query
quality.description: Detects queries using non-deterministic functions (NOW, RAND) in filters/logic.
quality.impact: Non-deterministic queries are hard to test and reproduce. They can cause flaky tests and unpredictable behavior in production if results depend on the exact millisecond of execution.
quality.fix_guidance: Pass time as a parameter from the application layer. Use fixed seeds for random functions. Ensure query results are predictable for the same input state.

quality.name: Pagination Without ORDER BY
quality.description: Detects LIMIT/OFFSET usage without an explicit ORDER BY clause.
quality.impact: SQL does not guarantee row order without ORDER BY. Without it, pagination can return the same row on multiple pages or skip rows entirely, leading to UI bugs.
quality.fix_guidance: Always add ORDER BY when using LIMIT/OFFSET. Ensure the sort key is unique (e.g., include ID) to guarantee stable sorting.

quality.name: Hardcoded Test Data
quality.description: Detects obvious test data patterns (test, dummy, fake, temp, asdf, qwerty) in queries.
quality.impact: Leftover test data markers in production queries indicate poor release hygiene. They can accidentally filter out real data or expose test logic to users.
quality.fix_guidance: Remove test data filters from production queries. Use proper environment configuration to separate test and production logic.

quality.name: Technical Debt Marker
quality.description: Detects TODO or FIXME markers in query comments, indicating unresolved issues.
quality.impact: TODO/FIXME markers represent known bugs or missing features that haven't been tracked in an issue tracker. They often rot and become obsolete while the issues remain.
quality.fix_guidance: Resolve the underlying issue or move the task to a formal issue tracking system. Unresolved notes in code hinder long-term maintainability.

quality.name: Permanent Temporary Table
quality.description: Detects CREATE TEMP TABLE without a corresponding DROP TABLE in the same unit.
quality.impact: Temporary tables that aren't dropped consume memory and disk space in the temporary tablespace. Over time, they can cause disk full errors and slow down the database.
quality.fix_guidance: Always DROP temporary tables as soon as they are no longer needed. Use 'ON COMMIT DROP' if supported by your database.

quality.name: Deprecated SQL_CALC_FOUND_ROWS
quality.description: SQL_CALC_FOUND_ROWS is deprecated as of MySQL 8.0.17. It forces the server to compute the full result set size even when LIMIT is used, causing unnecessary overhead.
quality.impact: SQL_CALC_FOUND_ROWS disables LIMIT optimisations and forces a full table scan to count all matching rows. It is deprecated and will be removed in a future MySQL version.
quality.fix_guidance: Replace with two separate queries: one with LIMIT for the page and one COUNT(*) query for the total. Use covering indexes to make the COUNT(*) query fast.

quality.name: STRAIGHT_JOIN Hint
quality.description: STRAIGHT_JOIN forces MySQL to read tables in query order, overriding optimizer.
quality.impact: Forced join order may become suboptimal as data distribution changes.
quality.fix_guidance: Remove STRAIGHT_JOIN. Update statistics with ANALYZE TABLE.

quality.name: SET ANSI_NULLS OFF
quality.description: SET ANSI_NULLS OFF enables deprecated non-standard NULL comparison behavior.
quality.impact: Code relying on ANSI_NULLS OFF will break when the setting is removed.
quality.fix_guidance: Use IS NULL instead of = NULL. Remove SET ANSI_NULLS OFF.

quality.name: NVL in WHERE Clause
quality.description: NVL() in WHERE wraps the column in a function, preventing index usage.
quality.impact: NVL() makes the predicate non-SARGable.
quality.fix_guidance: Replace NVL(col, val) = x with (col = x OR col IS NULL).

quality.name: AUTOINCREMENT Overhead in SQLite
quality.description: SQLite's AUTOINCREMENT keyword adds CPU overhead by maintaining the sqlite_sequence table. Plain INTEGER PRIMARY KEY already auto-generates unique rowids without this overhead.
quality.impact: AUTOINCREMENT prevents rowid reuse and requires extra reads/writes to the sqlite_sequence table on every INSERT.
quality.fix_guidance: Use INTEGER PRIMARY KEY without AUTOINCREMENT. SQLite guarantees unique monotonically increasing rowids without it. AUTOINCREMENT only guarantees never-reused IDs, which is rarely needed.

quality.name: Deprecated Old-Style Type Cast
quality.description: DuckDB supports PostgreSQL-style :: casts but also allows old-style type(value) syntax which is less readable and may be confused with function calls.
quality.impact: Old-style casts are visually ambiguous with function calls and reduce code readability.
quality.fix_guidance: Use CAST(value AS type) or value::type instead of type(value).

quality.name: DO Block Without LANGUAGE
quality.description: PostgreSQL DO blocks should specify LANGUAGE explicitly. Without it, PostgreSQL defaults to plpgsql but this is implicit and reduces readability.
quality.impact: Implicit language defaults reduce code clarity and can cause errors if the default language changes or if plpgsql is not loaded.
quality.fix_guidance: Add LANGUAGE plpgsql: DO $$ BEGIN ... END $$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

quality.name: DISTSTYLE ALL on Large Table
quality.description: DISTSTYLE ALL copies the entire table to every node. This is only appropriate for small dimension tables. On large tables it wastes storage and slows down writes.
quality.impact: Every INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE replicates to all nodes. For large tables this multiplies write time and storage by the cluster size.
quality.fix_guidance: Use DISTSTYLE ALL only for small dimension tables (<1M rows). For large tables use DISTSTYLE KEY or DISTSTYLE EVEN.

quality.name: ORDER BY Without LIMIT on ClickHouse
quality.description: ORDER BY without LIMIT on ClickHouse requires gathering and sorting all data on a single node. For distributed tables this causes massive memory usage.
quality.impact: All data is gathered to one node for global sorting. This can exhaust memory and crash the query.
quality.fix_guidance: Add LIMIT to bound results. Or use ORDER BY with LIMIT BY for top-N per group patterns.

quality.name: FLATTEN Without Explicit Path
quality.description: LATERAL FLATTEN without explicit path or input parameter relies on implicit column resolution, which can break if the schema changes.
